# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**feat: message pool allocator**

Todo #1 picked up: decouple "get a message" from "send it"
with a message pool — self-describing attach-validated
region, fixed-size cache-aligned buffers, intrusive LIFO
free-stack (single-popper Treiber, validated pops), one
owning allocator, anyone frees. Strive for simplicity: the
smallest API that lets us hone the shape and gives iiac-perf
a baseline —

- buffer header is the next-link alone for now; provenance
  (pool id) and any length/type tag land with the
  descriptor-queue cycle, via a pool layout_version bump
  (conscious deferral of the design doc's settle-early note).
- API mirrors the ring: `Pool::init` / `unsafe Pool::attach`,
  a single allocator handle, typed zerocopy access to
  buffers.

Ladder:

- 0.6.0-0 chore: open message pool cycle (current)
- 0.6.0-1 feat: message pool layout + init/attach
- 0.6.0-2 feat: message pool alloc/free
- 0.6.0-3 test: message pool protocol tests
- 0.6.0 feat: message pool allocator (close-out)

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Descriptor queues: carry `(pool id, buffer offset)`
   descriptors over the existing SPSC ring so any message
   travels over any queue; per-process pool registry to
   resolve ids
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).
2. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
3. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

content.text:
`0.6.0-1 feat: message pool layout + init/attach` is ready to commit. What landed:

**`src/pool.rs`** (new, ~250 lines) — the pool's region layer, deliberately mirroring the ring's discipline so the two read as siblings:

- `PoolHeader`, two cache lines: line 0 is cold geometry (`magic` "ZCP1", `layout_version` 1, `buf_size`, `buf_count`, `cache_line`), line 1 is the `free_head` alone — it's the CAS-contended word, so it owns its line. All fields atomic, same hostile-peer rationale as the ring. Const-asserted at 128 bytes.
- **Zero per-buffer overhead**: the intrusive next-link is the first word of a *free* buffer (meaningful only while free); an allocated buffer carries no header at all. `NIL = u32::MAX` terminates the stack, which is why `buf_count < u32::MAX` is a geometry rule. No power-of-two constraint — a stack never masks.
- `Pool::init` — validates geometry (u64 region-size math, same 32-bit wrap guard as the ring), links every buffer `i → i+1`, head = 0, magic-last `Release` handshake. `unsafe Pool::attach` — magic `Acquire` first, then layout version, cache line, geometry snapshot; its safety contract states the single-popper rule: at most one attached handle allocates, any handle frees.
- `buf_size()` / `buf_count()` accessors; private `link_of`/`buf_ptr` helpers that `-2`'s alloc/free will reuse.

**`src/lib.rs`** — `pool` module wired in and re-exported; `Error` gains `BadBufSize` / `BadBufCount` (one shared error enum, doc comment updated).

**Notes/version** — ladder `-1` flipped `(done)`; your flagged backfill done: `Commits: [[15]]` on the cycle's chores section with the now-permanent `0.6.0-0` SHA `1d6d3889…`; version-of-record `0.6.0-1`.

Validation: fmt, clippy `-D warnings`, cargo test, and full Miri all pass (tests exercising the pool itself come at `-3` per the ladder). Review when ready.

message.content: In pool.rs PoolHeader what is cache_line?

content.text:
It's the recorded cache-line size the region was built with — a copy of the crate's `CACHE_LINE` constant (currently 64) stored into the header at `Pool::init`, and checked by `Pool::attach`, which returns `Error::BadCacheLine` on mismatch (src/pool.rs:169).

Why it's worth a header word: `CACHE_LINE` isn't just a performance hint here, it's a **layout parameter**. It determines the header's size and padding (`CacheAligned` wrappers), the buffer-size granularity rule, and buffer alignment. Two processes sharing a pool region must agree on it or they'd compute different offsets for the same bytes — silent corruption, not slowdown.

Today every build uses 64, so the check can never fail between honest peers. It exists for the future in the Ideas list: per-target or configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple M-series false-sharing pitch, smaller for cache-less MCUs). When that lands, a build compiled with 128 attaching to a region built with 64 gets a clean `BadCacheLine` error instead of undetected layout disagreement. The ring's `Header` has carried the same field since its layout v2 — the pool inherits the discipline (assume nothing, verify at the boundary).

message.content: free_head_idx how about:  next_buf_idx. Push back it you don't like it

content.text:
I'll push back — I'd keep `free_head_idx`. Three reasons:

1. **`next_buf_idx` describes only the pop side.** From the allocator's view it is "the next buffer you'll get," but from a freer's view it's *where you push*. The field serves both operations; "head of the free-stack" is the neutral name that fits both, while "next buf" reads like a bump-allocator cursor marching through the array in sequence — which actively misleads about the data structure behind it.

2. **It collides with the per-buffer links.** Each free buffer's first word is already its *next*-link (`link_of` — "index of the next free buffer"). If the header field is also named "next," the design's two distinct roles — the head of the stack versus a node's next pointer — share one word, and the code stops telling you which is which.

3. **Convention carries information.** "Free head" is the canonical name for this exact thing in free-list/Treiber-stack literature; a reader who's seen any allocator recognizes `free_head_idx` on sight, including the LIFO implication that `next_buf_idx` erases.

The name spells out the structure: it's the head (so a stack), of the free list (so only free buffers), holding an index (your earlier improvement). If the itch is that "head" is opaque, `first_free_idx` would be my second choice — it keeps "free" and stays direction-neutral — but `free_head_idx` says the most per character. Keep it?

message.content: link_of how about get_next_buf_idx or next_buf_idx, at some point we'll need the ref pointer too?

content.text:
`next_buf_idx` works — dropping the `get_` per Rust convention (getters aren't `get_`-prefixed). And it's nicely symmetric with the header's `free_head_idx`: both are `AtomicU32` cells holding a buffer index. Note it returns the atomic *cell* (`&AtomicU32`), not the value — the doc comment keeps saying so.

On the ref pointer: that's already there as the private `buf_ptr(idx)`. It stays raw on purpose — in `-2` the alloc guard will mint `&mut T` from it per access via `Deref`, exactly like the ring's `WriteSlot` (the raw-pointer-not-reference-field lesson Miri taught in 0.3.0-6), with `check_type` validating `T` against `buf_size`.

content.text:
No pushback this time — you've found a real flaw my earlier defense missed: **"free" is a verb as well as an adjective.** `pool.header.free_head_idx.store(...)` genuinely parses as "free the head idx" — an action — and at a store site that misreading is live, exactly as you experienced. My earlier case was for keeping the word "free"; it didn't consider the verb trap.

The fix is a name where "free" can only be an adjective: **`first_free_idx`** — "index of the first free buffer." No verb reading ("first" blocks it), keeps "free" and "idx", and it's a familiar allocator idiom. It was my stated second choice earlier and the verb problem promotes it to first.

message.content: Why attach after init, why not have init imply attaching?

content.text:
`init` doesn't need a follow-up attach — it already implies it. `Pool::init` returns a fully usable `Pool` handle (same for `Ring::init`): the initializing side never calls `attach` at all. The two functions aren't sequential steps; they're **two doors into the same handle** for two different parties:

- **`init`** — for the region's creator. It has the memory as `&mut [u8]`, so the borrow checker *proves* exclusive access: nobody else can be watching while the header is half-written. That proof is what lets `init` be a safe function, and it returns the ready handle directly.
- **`attach`** — for everyone else: the other process (or a later re-mapping) joining a region someone else already initialized. It can't take `&mut [u8]`, because a genuinely shared `MAP_SHARED` mapping is never exclusively borrowed — a peer may be writing at any moment. Hence the raw pointer, the header validation (magic/version/geometry, which `init` doesn't need — it just wrote those), and the `unsafe` contract about mapping liveness.

So the asymmetry in signatures is the design: `init` = create + attach fused, safe by exclusivity; `attach` = join, unsafe because only the caller can vouch for the mapping.

The tempting third shape — one `open_or_create` that inits if the region looks fresh, attaches otherwise — is the one I'd argue against. "Looks fresh" means checking `magic`, and two processes racing through that check both conclude "fresh" and both initialize: a TOCTOU race the current handshake deliberately doesn't have to handle, because *who initializes* is the caller's topology decision (embedded: startup code before spawning; Linux: the creator of the shm fd inits before passing it). Making that race safe would need a CAS claim on `magic` and defined loser semantics — machinery that buys convenience, not capability. If it's ever wanted, it belongs as a third door built on the existing two, not a replacement for them.

lastPrompt: Why attach after init, why not have init imply attaching?

message.content: How about "use" instead of "attach"?

content.text:
`use` is off the table on hard grounds: it's a Rust keyword, so the method would have to be written `r#use` — `Pool::r#use(...)` at every call site — which no API should inflict.

Setting the keyword aside, I'd still defend `attach` over its legal cousins:

- It names the relationship accurately: the region already exists and is live; this side is *attaching itself* to it. That's exactly the POSIX shared-memory verb too (`shmat` = "shared memory attach"), so systems programmers arrive pre-trained.
- `open` (iceoryx2's choice) implies a resource being located and acquired — but we take a pointer to memory the caller already mapped; nothing is opened.
- `join` reads socially fine but has no systems lineage; `map` is wrong (the caller did the mapping; we validate it).

And there's a repo-specific cost: `attach` is load-bearing across the existing surface — `Ring::attach`, the design doc's init/attach handshake sections, `BadMagic`/`BadCacheLine` doc references, the "attach validates it" idiom in comments. Renaming just the pool's door would break the ring/pool symmetry we've been deliberately maintaining; renaming both is a wide diff for a synonym swap.

Keep `attach`?

message.content:
good reason, attach for now but nothing is in stone :)
How do I get ownership of a buf so I can use it?

content.text:
Today you can't — that's deliberately the next rung: `0.6.0-2 feat: message pool alloc/free`. The `-1` on the table only builds and validates the region. But since the API shape is exactly what this cycle exists to hone, here's the shape I intend for `-2`:

```rust
// allocator side (the one owning handle):
let mut msg: BufSlot<'_, MyMsg> = pool.alloc::<MyMsg>()?;   // pops first_free_idx, validated
msg.field = 42;                                             // DerefMut, in place
// ... hold it as long as you like, alloc more meanwhile ...
msg.free();                                                 // pushes back onto the free-stack
```

- **`alloc` pops the free-stack**: load `first_free_idx`, bounds-check it (untrusted), read that buffer's `next_buf_idx`, bounds-check *that*, CAS the head over. Returns `Err(Exhausted)` on `NIL`. Typed via the same zerocopy bounds and `check_type` as the ring's slots.
- **The guard borrows the pool *shared*, not `&mut`** — this is the load-bearing decision. The ring's `WriteSlot` holds `&mut Producer`, so one reservation at a time; if `BufSlot` did that, we'd have rebuilt the exact coupling this cycle exists to remove. Instead many `BufSlot`s live concurrently; per-buffer exclusivity comes from the protocol (each pop yields a distinct index), not the borrow checker.
- **Ownership semantics**: the guard *is* your ownership token. `free(self)` returns the buffer (a CAS push any handle/process may do); dropping without freeing leaks the buffer until the pool is re-initialized — documented, like the ring's abandon-on-drop but with the opposite consequence. "Send it" (the descriptor-queue cycle) will later consume the guard into a bare index and the receiver will reconstruct access on its side.
- `alloc` takes `&mut self` on the allocator handle to enforce single-popper in-process; `free` needs only `&self`.

That last asymmetry echoes your original insight: getting is a private act of the one allocator, releasing is open to the world.

If that shape reads right, the natural order is: commit `-1` as reviewed, then I implement `-2` on top. Shall I write the `-1` description?

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**feat: message pool allocator**

Todo #1 picked up: decouple "get a message" from "send it"
with a message pool — self-describing attach-validated
region, fixed-size cache-aligned buffers, intrusive LIFO
free-stack (single-popper Treiber, validated pops), one
owning allocator, anyone frees. Strive for simplicity: the
smallest API that lets us hone the shape and gives iiac-perf
a baseline —

- buffer header is the next-link alone for now; provenance
  (pool id) and any length/type tag land with the
  descriptor-queue cycle, via a pool layout_version bump
  (conscious deferral of the design doc's settle-early note).
- API mirrors the ring: `Pool::init` / `unsafe Pool::attach`,
  a single allocator handle, typed zerocopy access to
  buffers.

Ladder:

- 0.6.0-0 chore: open message pool cycle (done)
- 0.6.0-1 feat: message pool layout + init/attach (done)
- 0.6.0-2 feat: message pool alloc/free
- 0.6.0-3 test: message pool protocol tests
- 0.6.0 feat: message pool allocator (close-out)

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Descriptor queues: carry `(pool id, buffer offset)`
   descriptors over the existing SPSC ring so any message
   travels over any queue; per-process pool registry to
   resolve ids
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).
2. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
3. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- feat: ring buffer user line + blocking contract [[5]]
- docs: messaging layer design (pools + queues) [[6]]

# References

[5]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-ring-buffer-user-line--blocking-contract
[6]: chores/chores-01.md#docs-messaging-layer-design-pools--queues

content.text:
`0.6.0-2 feat: message pool alloc/free` is ready to commit — the API is exactly the shape we agreed:

**`Pool::alloc<T>(&mut self) -> Result<BufSlot<'a, T>, Exhausted>`** (src/pool.rs:203)
- Validated Treiber pop: head `Acquire`-loaded and bounds-checked, its next-link bounds-checked too, then CAS the head over; corruption fails toward `Exhausted` (the ring's fail-toward-`Full` philosophy), never an out-of-bounds buffer. A racing free just retries the CAS; single popper means no ABA.
- `&mut self` is only the in-process single-popper token — the returned guard borrows the *region* lifetime, not the pool handle, so allocs stack up freely.

**`BufSlot<T>`** — the ownership token: `Deref`/`DerefMut` mint `&T`/`&mut T` per access from a raw pointer (the ring guards' Miri-taught aliasing pattern), `free(self)` is the MPSC push (link store then `Release` CAS, retry on contention), dropping without `free` leaks the buffer (documented). `Send` when `T: Send`, so a holder can free from another thread — the topology the design calls for. `zerocopy` bounds + `check_type` against `buf_size` as everywhere else.

Bookkeeping rode along: `Commits: [[16]]` backfilled with the `-1` SHA `2b09687d…`, ladder `-2` flipped, version `0.6.0-2`.

Validation: fmt, clippy `-D warnings`, tests, and Miri all pass — though note the 7 passing tests are still the ring's; the pool's own coverage is next rung (`-3 test: message pool protocol tests`), so alloc/free is currently compiler-and-Miri-checked but not yet exercised. If you'd rather see the tests before committing this, I can pull `-3` forward; otherwise review and I'll write the description.

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**feat: message pool allocator**

Todo #1 picked up: decouple "get a message" from "send it"
with a message pool — self-describing attach-validated
region, fixed-size cache-aligned buffers, intrusive LIFO
free-stack (single-popper Treiber, validated pops), one
owning allocator, anyone frees. Strive for simplicity: the
smallest API that lets us hone the shape and gives iiac-perf
a baseline —

- buffer header is the next-link alone for now; provenance
  (pool id) and any length/type tag land with the
  descriptor-queue cycle, via a pool layout_version bump
  (conscious deferral of the design doc's settle-early note).
- API mirrors the ring: `Pool::init` / `unsafe Pool::attach`,
  a single allocator handle, typed zerocopy access to
  buffers.

Ladder:

- 0.6.0-0 chore: open message pool cycle (done)
- 0.6.0-1 feat: message pool layout + init/attach (done)
- 0.6.0-2 feat: message pool alloc/free (done)
- 0.6.0-3 test: message pool protocol tests
- 0.6.0 feat: message pool allocator (close-out)

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Descriptor queues: carry `(pool id, buffer offset)`
   descriptors over the existing SPSC ring so any message
   travels over any queue; per-process pool registry to
   resolve ids
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).
2. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
3. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

content.text:
Ladder updated — `0.6.0-3 docs: message pool usage model` inserted with a scope sketch (roles, lifecycle states, per-state access rights, and what "send" means today), tests pushed to `-4`. Two things the doc step will force us to agree on, flagging them now:

1. **What "send" means this cycle**: with no descriptor queues yet, transferring a message = moving the `BufSlot` (Rust move semantics; `Send` lets it cross threads). Cross-process transfer, and reconstructing buffer access from a bare index on the receiving side, are explicitly *not* in this cycle — that's the descriptor-queue API. The tests at `-4` will exercise exactly what the doc permits: alloc here, free there, many live buffers, exhaustion, ring and pool composed in-process.
2. **Lifecycle ownership**: free (pool owns, nobody touches) → allocated (guard holder has exclusive read/write) → freed (back to pool). "In-flight" exists in the model but is vacant until descriptor queues land — documenting the placeholder keeps the state machine honest.

The `-2` code is still uncommitted; the ladder edit rides with it. Want me to write the `-2` description now (alloc/free + ladder insert), with the usage-model doc following as `-3`?

message.content: yes

content.text:
Description for `0.6.0-2`:

```
feat: message pool alloc/free

The decoupled ownership API: alloc pops the first free
buffer as a BufSlot owned until free — the guard does not
borrow the pool, so any number of allocated buffers live at
once, and getting a message implies nothing about when it
is sent or freed. Validated Treiber ops: single-popper pop
(no ABA), MPSC Release push, corruption fails toward
Exhausted, never out of bounds.

- src/pool.rs
  - Exhausted: alloc failure (empty or peer-corrupted
    stack — fail-safe like the ring's Full)
  - Pool::alloc<T>: Acquire + bounds-checked head and
    next-link, CAS pop; &mut self as the in-process
    single-popper token; returns BufSlot<'a, T> tied to
    the region lifetime, not the handle borrow
  - BufSlot<T>: Deref/DerefMut mint refs per access from a
    raw pointer (ring guards' aliasing pattern); free(self)
    re-links the first word and Release-CAS pushes; drop
    without free leaks (documented); Send when T: Send
  - module doc: single-popper / MPSC-push usage contract
- src/lib.rs: re-export BufSlot, Exhausted
- notes/todo.md: ladder -2 (done); insert -3 docs: message
  pool usage model, tests shift to -4

  feat SHA) into the message-pool section
- Cargo.toml, Cargo.lock: version-of-record 0.6.0-2
```

Approve and I'll run `vc-x1 push main`.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
Message pool region layer, mirroring the ring's discipline:
all-atomic two-line PoolHeader (cold geometry; contended
first_free_idx alone on line 1), magic-last Release/Acquire
init/attach handshake, geometry snapshot so per-op paths
never re-read peer-writable fields. The free-stack is
intrusive with zero per-buffer overhead: a free buffer's
first word is its next_buf_idx link; alloc/free land next.

- src/pool.rs: new — PoolHeader (magic "ZCP1", layout v1,
  buf_size, buf_count, cache_line_size, first_free_idx),
  Pool::init (validates geometry in u64, links buffers
  i -> i+1, last -> NIL, head 0), unsafe Pool::attach
  (single-popper contract in its safety docs), buf_size /
  buf_count accessors, private next_buf_idx / buf_ptr
  helpers for the coming alloc/free
- src/lib.rs
  - pool module wired in; Pool, PoolHeader re-exported
  - Error gains BadBufSize / BadBufCount; doc updated to
    cover both region kinds
  - Header.cache_line renamed cache_line_size for pool
    symmetry (field rename only, no layout change)
- notes/ring-buffer-design.md: cache_line_size rename synced
  in the Header snippet and CACHE_LINE bullet
- notes/chores/chores-01.md: backfill [[15]] (the 0.6.0-0
  chore SHA) into the message-pool section
- notes/todo.md: ladder -0 (done), -1 (done)
- Cargo.toml, Cargo.lock: version-of-record 0.6.0-1

3. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

input.new_string:
### Usage model: roles and buffer lifecycle

Who may do what, per object and per buffer state. The tests
exercise exactly what this model permits; anything outside
it is a contract violation even where the compiler cannot
reject it.

**Roles.** Roles are per *object*, and one thread may hold
several roles across objects (a typical sender is one pool's
allocator and one ring's producer):

- **Ring** — exactly one producer and one consumer, ever
  (SPSC): in-process, `split()` hands out each endpoint
  once; cross-process, one producing and one consuming
  process by contract. An endpoint reserves at most one
  slot at a time (guard holds the endpoint borrow).
- **Pool** — exactly one *allocator* at a time (the
  single-popper contract on [`Pool::attach`]; in-process,
  `alloc` takes `&mut self`), and **any number of freers**:
  whoever holds a `BufSlot` may free it, from any thread or
  process attached to the pool.

**Buffer lifecycle.** A pool buffer is always in exactly one
state, with one party allowed to touch it:

- **free** — on the free-stack; the pool protocol owns it.
  Nobody reads or writes it except through alloc/free
  machinery (its first word is the next-link).
- **allocated** — popped by `alloc`, owned by the `BufSlot`
  holder: exclusive read/write through the guard, held as
  long as desired, moved between threads freely (`Send`).
  No other party — including the pool — may touch the
  bytes.
- **in-flight** — *placeholder, vacant this cycle*: after a
  send over a descriptor queue the sender must no longer
  touch the buffer and the receiver owns it on receipt.
  Until descriptor queues exist there is no in-flight
  state.
- **freed** — `BufSlot::free` pushes it back; ownership
  returns to the pool protocol the instant the CAS lands.
  Use-after-free of the guard is unrepresentable (`free`
  consumes it); dropping without `free` leaks the buffer.

**What "send" means this cycle.** Transferring a message =
moving its `BufSlot` (an ordinary Rust move; `Send` lets it
cross threads). The ring and the pool do not yet compose:
a `BufSlot` cannot travel through a ring slot, and no API
reconstructs buffer access from a bare index — both arrive
with the descriptor-queue cycle. Ring messages this cycle
are by-value `T`s in slots, as before.

**Trust.** Unchanged from the ring: every word in shared
memory is untrusted input. The pool validates the head and
next-link at every pop and fails toward `Exhausted`; a
hostile peer can degrade service (garbage messages, lost
buffers, spurious exhaustion), never cause UB on this side.

### Overflow FIFO (future)

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**feat: message pool allocator**

Todo #1 picked up: decouple "get a message" from "send it"
with a message pool — self-describing attach-validated
region, fixed-size cache-aligned buffers, intrusive LIFO
free-stack (single-popper Treiber, validated pops), one
owning allocator, anyone frees. Strive for simplicity: the
smallest API that lets us hone the shape and gives iiac-perf
a baseline —

- buffer header is the next-link alone for now; provenance
  (pool id) and any length/type tag land with the
  descriptor-queue cycle, via a pool layout_version bump
  (conscious deferral of the design doc's settle-early note).
- API mirrors the ring: `Pool::init` / `unsafe Pool::attach`,
  a single allocator handle, typed zerocopy access to
  buffers.

Ladder:

- 0.6.0-0 chore: open message pool cycle (done)
- 0.6.0-1 feat: message pool layout + init/attach (done)
- 0.6.0-2 feat: message pool alloc/free (done)
- 0.6.0-3 docs: message pool usage model — roles (ring:
  one producer + one consumer; pool: one allocator + any
  freers), buffer lifecycle states (free → allocated →
  in-flight → freed) and who may touch a buffer in each,
  what "send" means before descriptor queues exist (current)
- 0.6.0-4 test: message pool protocol tests
- 0.6.0 feat: message pool allocator (close-out)

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Descriptor queues: carry `(pool id, buffer offset)`
   descriptors over the existing SPSC ring so any message
   travels over any queue; per-process pool registry to
   resolve ids
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).
2. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
3. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

content.text:
`0.6.0-3 docs: message pool usage model` is ready to commit — and since the point of this rung is agreement, here's the model it pins down (new `### Usage model: roles and buffer lifecycle` in the messaging-layer section):

**Roles** (per object; one thread may hold several across objects — a typical sender is a pool's allocator plus a ring's producer):
- **Ring**: exactly one producer, one consumer, ever; one reserved slot at a time per endpoint.
- **Pool**: exactly one allocator at a time (single-popper), any number of freers — whoever holds a `BufSlot` may free it from any thread or process.

**Buffer lifecycle** — always exactly one state, one permitted toucher:
- **free** — the pool protocol owns it; its first word is the next-link; nobody else touches it.
- **allocated** — the `BufSlot` holder has exclusive read/write, indefinitely, movable across threads.
- **in-flight** — *explicitly documented as vacant this cycle*: it exists in the model so the state machine is honest, but only descriptor queues will populate it.
- **freed** — ownership snaps back to the pool at the free-CAS; use-after-free is unrepresentable (`free` consumes the guard), drop-without-free leaks.

**"Send" this cycle** = moving the `BufSlot` (ordinary Rust move / `Send`). Stated plainly: ring and pool do not yet compose — no `BufSlot` through a ring slot, no reconstruction from a bare index; ring messages remain by-value `T`s.

**Trust** — unchanged doctrine: shared words are untrusted input; hostile peers degrade service, never cause UB.

The section intro also now records the pool half as as-built (0.6.0 cycle) rather than "not yet built." Bookkeeping rode along: `[[17]]` backfilled with the `-2` SHA, ladder flipped, version `0.6.0-3`.

The `-4` tests will be written directly against this model: roles enforced, lifecycle transitions exercised (including cross-thread free and exhaustion), nothing outside it. If the model reads right, I'll write the description.

notes/README.md
README.md
# zc-ring-x1

Zero copy ring buffer experiment 1

This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
a bot to help develop the code.

The beginnings of a tool to help is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)

## Overview

A `no_std` SPSC (single-producer / single-consumer) ring buffer
over a caller-provided memory region, moving typed messages with
zero copying at the boundary — the producer writes through a
`&mut T` directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads through
a `&T` in place. Typed views are validated casts via
[zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy); the same `#[repr(C)]`
layout works between threads and, via shared memory, between
processes. The full design — requirements, memory layout, index
scheme, API, validation — lives in
[notes/ring-buffer-design.md](notes/ring-buffer-design.md), kept
in sync with `src/`.

- M slots × N bytes, both caller-chosen: M a power of two, N a
  cache-line multiple, every slot cache-line aligned.
- Free-running `AtomicU32` indices, masked only at slot access;
  no sacrificial slot; lock-free with Acquire/Release pairing.
- Guard API: `reserve_slot::<T>()` on either endpoint — write →
  `commit()` on the producer side, read → `release()` on the
  consumer side; dropping a guard abandons cleanly.
- IPC-ready: one contiguous region, no internal pointers, an
  init/attach handshake (magic published last, Release), and an
  all-atomic header so a misbehaving peer can corrupt data but
  never cause UB.
- An app-owned scratch line in the header (`user()` on both
  endpoints, 16 `AtomicU32`s): the crate zeroes it at init and
  never touches it again — a shared-memory home for app wakeup
  protocols; the crate itself never blocks or spins.
- Validated by tests (including a threaded stress and a
  u32-index-wrap proof) and [Miri](https://github.com/rust-lang/miri)

A `no_std` SPSC (single-producer / single-consumer) ring buffer
over a caller-provided memory region, moving typed messages with
zero copying at the boundary — the producer writes through a
`&mut T` directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads through
a `&T` in place. Typed views are validated casts via
[zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy); the same `#[repr(C)]`
layout works between threads and, via shared memory, between
processes. The full design — requirements, memory layout, index
scheme, API, validation — lives in
[notes/ring-buffer-design.md](notes/ring-buffer-design.md), kept
in sync with `src/`.

- M slots × N bytes, both caller-chosen: M a power of two, N a
  cache-line multiple, every slot cache-line aligned.
- Free-running `AtomicU32` indices, masked only at slot access;
  no sacrificial slot; lock-free with Acquire/Release pairing.
- Guard API: `reserve_slot::<T>()` on either endpoint — write →
  `commit()` on the producer side, read → `release()` on the
  consumer side; dropping a guard abandons cleanly.
- IPC-ready: one contiguous region, no internal pointers, an
  init/attach handshake (magic published last, Release), and an
  all-atomic header so a misbehaving peer can corrupt data but
  never cause UB.
- An app-owned scratch line in the header (`user()` on both
  endpoints, 16 `AtomicU32`s): the crate zeroes it at init and
  never touches it again — a shared-memory home for app wakeup
  protocols; the crate itself never blocks or spins.
- Validated by tests (including a threaded stress and a
  u32-index-wrap proof) and [Miri](https://github.com/rust-lang/miri)

content.content:
u32-index-wrap proof) and [Miri](https://github.com/rust-lang/miri)
  on the non-threaded suite.

```rust
use zc_ring_x1::Ring;
use zerocopy::{FromBytes, Immutable, IntoBytes, KnownLayout};

#[derive(FromBytes, IntoBytes, KnownLayout, Immutable)]
#[repr(C)]
struct Msg {
    seq: u64,
    val: u64,
}

// Cache-line-aligned region: 256 B header + 4 slots × 64 B.
#[repr(C, align(64))]
struct Region([u8; 512]);
let mut region = Region([0; 512]);

let (mut producer, mut consumer) =
    Ring::init(&mut region.0, 64, 4).unwrap().split();

let mut slot = producer.reserve_slot::<Msg>().unwrap();
slot.seq = 1;
slot.val = 42;
slot.commit(); // publish to the consumer

let msg = consumer.reserve_slot::<Msg>().unwrap();
assert_eq!(msg.val, 42);
msg.release(); // slot is free for reuse
```

Status: an experiment. SPSC only; attaching to an existing
shared-memory region is `unsafe` (see `Ring::attach`); planned
hardening and follow-ons are tracked in
[notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md).

## Testing

- `cargo test` — the full suite, including a threaded stress
  test and a u32-index-wrap test.
- `cargo run --example readme` — the Overview example above
  (committed as [examples/readme.rs](examples/readme.rs) so
  `cargo clippy --all-targets` keeps it compiling against the
  real API).
- `cargo +nightly miri test` — the full suite under
  [Miri](https://github.com/rust-lang/miri), which checks the
  `unsafe` code against the memory model; it has caught two
  real Stacked Borrows bugs here (an init retag in 0.3.0-4, a
  guard aliasing race in 0.3.0-6 — the latter only visible
  with the threaded test included). One-time setup:
  `rustup component add --toolchain nightly miri`. The
  threaded stress test runs a reduced message count under
  Miri (interpreted spin loops are slow).

## Releasing

**`cargo install` lockfile gotcha.** If you install your project's
binary with `cargo install --path .`, also pass `--locked`. By
default `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock` and re-resolves
dependencies from scratch, so it can silently build the binary from
a different — possibly broken — dependency graph than `cargo build`
/ `cargo test` ran against.

| command | reads `Cargo.lock`? | re-resolves? |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `cargo build` / `test` / `run`  | yes     | only if `Cargo.toml` changed |
| `cargo build --locked` (etc.)   | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |
| `cargo install` (default)       | **no**  | always, from scratch |
| `cargo install --locked`        | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |

There's no stable cargo config knob to make `--locked` the default
for `cargo install`, so the discipline lives in the per-commit cargo
cycle and shell aliases (e.g. `alias ci='cargo install --locked'`).

The trade-off is predictable-as-tested (`--locked`) vs picking up
upstream fixes via fresh resolves (default). Either is defensible —
pick what fits your project, then edit this section and the
per-commit cargo cycle to match. Background:
[cargo#7169](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/7169),
[cargo#9436](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/9436).

## jj Tips for Git Users

This project uses [Jujutsu (jj)](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/)
alongside git. New to jj? See
[Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)'s
[Jujutsu tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial).

Repo-specific how-tos — initial commit, pushing, modifying and
force-pushing a commit, revsets, and a useful-commands reference —
live in [notes/jj-tips.md](notes/jj-tips.md).

## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers

Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo via an
`ochid` (Other Change ID) trailer — the defining mechanism of the
dual-repo convention. For the full definition — trailer syntax,
the example shape, per-commit mechanics, and `.vc-config.toml` —
see
[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers).

## Contributing

Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
canonical in [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) (which `CLAUDE.md` imports)
and [notes/cycle-protocol.md](notes/cycle-protocol.md):

- [Cycle numbering](notes/cycle-protocol.md#numbering) — the
  `X.Y.Z-N` phase-suffix convention (Preparation / Work /
  Close-out).
- [Commit description](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
  — Conventional Commits title rules (no version suffix,
  distinct per step) and per-repo body shape.
- [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
  the cargo cycle plus the Work and Commit-Description review
  gates.
- [Code Conventions](AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.

Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
details in `notes/chores/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific
formatting rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).

Status: an experiment. SPSC only; attaching to an existing
shared-memory region is `unsafe` (see `Ring::attach`); planned
hardening and follow-ons are tracked in
[notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md).

## Message pool

The companion allocation primitive: fixed-size
cache-line-aligned buffers over a caller-provided region,
decoupling "get a message" from "send it" — allocate a
buffer, hold it as long as you like, free (or eventually
send) it whenever. Design and roadmap:
[Messaging layer: pools and descriptor queues](notes/ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).

- Intrusive LIFO free-stack with zero per-buffer overhead: a
  *free* buffer's first word is its next-link; an allocated
  buffer carries no header at all.
- One allocator, any freers: `alloc` is a single-popper
  Treiber pop (`&mut self`, no ABA); `BufSlot::free` is an
  MPSC push callable wherever the guard was moved.
- `BufSlot` does not borrow the pool — any number of
  allocated buffers live at once; dropping without `free`
  leaks the buffer (documented, like the ring's abandon but
  with the opposite consequence).
- Free-stack words are untrusted shared memory: pops are
  bounds-checked and corruption degrades to `Exhausted`,
  never out-of-bounds access.
- Same init/attach handshake, all-atomic header, and
  hostile-peer posture as the ring.

```rust
use zc_ring_x1::Pool;
use zerocopy::{FromBytes, IntoBytes, KnownLayout};

#[derive(FromBytes, IntoBytes, KnownLayout)]
#[repr(C)]
struct Msg {
    seq: u64,
    val: u64,
}

// Cache-line-aligned region: 128 B header + 4 bufs × 64 B.
#[repr(C, align(64))]
struct Region([u8; 384]);
let mut region = Region([0; 384]);

let mut pool = Pool::init(&mut region.0, 64, 4).unwrap();

let mut a = pool.alloc::<Msg>().unwrap();
let mut b = pool.alloc::<Msg>().unwrap(); // many live at once
a.seq = 1;
b.seq = 2;
b.free(); // any order, from any thread the guard moved to
a.free();
```

## Usage model: roles and buffer lifecycle

Who may do what, per object and per buffer state. The tests
exercise exactly what this model permits; anything outside it
is a contract violation even where the compiler cannot reject
it.

**Roles.** Roles are per *object*, and one thread may hold
several roles across objects (a typical sender is one pool's
allocator and one ring's producer):

- **Ring** — exactly one producer and one consumer, ever
  (SPSC): in-process, `split()` hands out each endpoint
  once; cross-process, one producing and one consuming
  process by contract. An endpoint reserves at most one
  slot at a time (guard holds the endpoint borrow).
- **Pool** — exactly one *allocator* at a time (the
  single-popper contract on `Pool::attach`; in-process,
  `alloc` takes `&mut self`), and **any number of freers**:
  whoever holds a `BufSlot` may free it, from any thread or
  process attached to the pool.

**Buffer lifecycle.** A pool buffer is always in exactly one
state, with one party allowed to touch it:

- **free** — on the free-stack; the pool protocol owns it.
  Nobody reads or writes it except through alloc/free
  machinery (its first word is the next-link).
- **allocated** — popped by `alloc`, owned by the `BufSlot`
  holder: exclusive read/write through the guard, held as
  long as desired, moved between threads freely (`Send`).
  No other party — including the pool — may touch the
  bytes.
- **in-flight** — *placeholder, vacant this cycle*: after a
  send over a descriptor queue the sender must no longer
  touch the buffer and the receiver owns it on receipt.
  Until descriptor queues exist there is no in-flight
  state.
- **freed** — `BufSlot::free` pushes it back; ownership
  returns to the pool protocol the instant the CAS lands.
  Use-after-free of the guard is unrepresentable (`free`
  consumes it); dropping without `free` leaks the buffer.

**What "send" means this cycle.** Transferring a message =
moving its `BufSlot` (an ordinary Rust move; `Send` lets it
cross threads). The ring and the pool do not yet compose:
a `BufSlot` cannot travel through a ring slot, and no API
reconstructs buffer access from a bare index — both arrive
with the descriptor-queue cycle. Ring messages this cycle
are by-value `T`s in slots, as before.

**Trust.** Unchanged from the ring: every word in shared
memory is untrusted input. The pool validates the head and
next-link at every pop and fails toward `Exhausted`; a
hostile peer can degrade service (garbage messages, lost
buffers, spurious exhaustion), never cause UB on this side.

## Testing

# zc-ring-x1

Zero copy ring buffer experiment 1

This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
a bot to help develop the code.

The beginnings of a tool to help is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)

## Overview

A `no_std` SPSC (single-producer / single-consumer) ring buffer
over a caller-provided memory region, moving typed messages with
zero copying at the boundary — the producer writes through a
`&mut T` directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads through
a `&T` in place. Typed views are validated casts via
[zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy); the same `#[repr(C)]`
layout works between threads and, via shared memory, between
processes. The full design — requirements, memory layout, index
scheme, API, validation — lives in
[notes/ring-buffer-design.md](notes/ring-buffer-design.md), kept
in sync with `src/`.

- M slots × N bytes, both caller-chosen: M a power of two, N a
  cache-line multiple, every slot cache-line aligned.
- Free-running `AtomicU32` indices, masked only at slot access;
  no sacrificial slot; lock-free with Acquire/Release pairing.
- Guard API: `reserve_slot::<T>()` on either endpoint — write →
  `commit()` on the producer side, read → `release()` on the
  consumer side; dropping a guard abandons cleanly.
- IPC-ready: one contiguous region, no internal pointers, an
  init/attach handshake (magic published last, Release), and an
  all-atomic header so a misbehaving peer can corrupt data but
  never cause UB.
- An app-owned scratch line in the header (`user()` on both
  endpoints, 16 `AtomicU32`s): the crate zeroes it at init and
  never touches it again — a shared-memory home for app wakeup
  protocols; the crate itself never blocks or spins.
- Validated by tests (including a threaded stress and a
  u32-index-wrap proof) and [Miri](https://github.com/rust-lang/miri)
  on the non-threaded suite.

```rust
use zc_ring_x1::Ring;
use zerocopy::{FromBytes, Immutable, IntoBytes, KnownLayout};

#[derive(FromBytes, IntoBytes, KnownLayout, Immutable)]
#[repr(C)]
struct Msg {
    seq: u64,
    val: u64,
}

// Cache-line-aligned region: 256 B header + 4 slots × 64 B.
#[repr(C, align(64))]

let (mut producer, mut consumer) =
    Ring::init(&mut region.0, 64, 4).unwrap().split();

let mut slot = producer.reserve_slot::<Msg>().unwrap();
slot.seq = 1;
slot.val = 42;
slot.commit(); // publish to the consumer

let msg = consumer.reserve_slot::<Msg>().unwrap();
assert_eq!(msg.val, 42);
msg.release(); // slot is free for reuse
```

hardening and follow-ons are tracked in

## Testing

- `cargo run --example readme` — the Overview example above

  `cargo clippy --all-targets` keeps it compiling against the
  real API).
- `cargo +nightly miri test` — the full suite under
  [Miri](https://github.com/rust-lang/miri), which checks the
  `unsafe` code against the memory model; it has caught two
  real Stacked Borrows bugs here (an init retag in 0.3.0-4, a
  guard aliasing race in 0.3.0-6 — the latter only visible
  with the threaded test included). One-time setup:
  `rustup component add --toolchain nightly miri`. The
  threaded stress test runs a reduced message count under
  Miri (interpreted spin loops are slow).

## Releasing

**`cargo install` lockfile gotcha.** If you install your project's
binary with `cargo install --path .`, also pass `--locked`. By
default `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock` and re-resolves
dependencies from scratch, so it can silently build the binary from
a different — possibly broken — dependency graph than `cargo build`
/ `cargo test` ran against.

| command | reads `Cargo.lock`? | re-resolves? |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `cargo build` / `test` / `run`  | yes     | only if `Cargo.toml` changed |
| `cargo build --locked` (etc.)   | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |
| `cargo install` (default)       | **no**  | always, from scratch |
| `cargo install --locked`        | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |

There's no stable cargo config knob to make `--locked` the default
for `cargo install`, so the discipline lives in the per-commit cargo
cycle and shell aliases (e.g. `alias ci='cargo install --locked'`).

The trade-off is predictable-as-tested (`--locked`) vs picking up
upstream fixes via fresh resolves (default). Either is defensible —
pick what fits your project, then edit this section and the
per-commit cargo cycle to match. Background:
[cargo#7169](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/7169),
[cargo#9436](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/9436).

## jj Tips for Git Users

This project uses [Jujutsu (jj)](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/)
alongside git. New to jj? See
[Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)'s
[Jujutsu tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial).

Repo-specific how-tos — initial commit, pushing, modifying and
force-pushing a commit, revsets, and a useful-commands reference —
live in [notes/jj-tips.md](notes/jj-tips.md).

## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers

Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo via an
`ochid` (Other Change ID) trailer — the defining mechanism of the
dual-repo convention. For the full definition — trailer syntax,
the example shape, per-commit mechanics, and `.vc-config.toml` —
see
[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers).

## Contributing

Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
canonical in [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) (which `CLAUDE.md` imports)
and [notes/cycle-protocol.md](notes/cycle-protocol.md):

- [Cycle numbering](notes/cycle-protocol.md#numbering) — the
  `X.Y.Z-N` phase-suffix convention (Preparation / Work /
  Close-out).
- [Commit description](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
  — Conventional Commits title rules (no version suffix,
  distinct per step) and per-repo body shape.
- [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
  the cargo cycle plus the Work and Commit-Description review
  gates.
- [Code Conventions](AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.

Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
details in `notes/chores/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific
formatting rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).

- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

### Contribution

Unless you explicitly state otherwise, any contribution intentionally submitted
for inclusion in the work by you, as defined in the Apache-2.0 license, shall
be dual licensed as above, without any additional terms or conditions.

input.old_string:
### Usage model: roles and buffer lifecycle

Who may do what, per object and per buffer state. The tests
exercise exactly what this model permits; anything outside
it is a contract violation even where the compiler cannot
reject it.

**Roles.** Roles are per *object*, and one thread may hold
several roles across objects (a typical sender is one pool's
allocator and one ring's producer):

- **Ring** — exactly one producer and one consumer, ever
  (SPSC): in-process, `split()` hands out each endpoint
  once; cross-process, one producing and one consuming
  process by contract. An endpoint reserves at most one
  slot at a time (guard holds the endpoint borrow).
- **Pool** — exactly one *allocator* at a time (the
  single-popper contract on `Pool::attach`; in-process,
  `alloc` takes `&mut self`), and **any number of freers**:
  whoever holds a `BufSlot` may free it, from any thread or
  process attached to the pool.

**Buffer lifecycle.** A pool buffer is always in exactly one
state, with one party allowed to touch it:

- **free** — on the free-stack; the pool protocol owns it.
  Nobody reads or writes it except through alloc/free
  machinery (its first word is the next-link).
- **allocated** — popped by `alloc`, owned by the `BufSlot`
  holder: exclusive read/write through the guard, held as
  long as desired, moved between threads freely (`Send`).
  No other party — including the pool — may touch the
  bytes.
- **in-flight** — *placeholder, vacant this cycle*: after a
  send over a descriptor queue the sender must no longer
  touch the buffer and the receiver owns it on receipt.
  Until descriptor queues exist there is no in-flight
  state.
- **freed** — `BufSlot::free` pushes it back; ownership
  returns to the pool protocol the instant the CAS lands.
  Use-after-free of the guard is unrepresentable (`free`
  consumes it); dropping without `free` leaks the buffer.

**What "send" means this cycle.** Transferring a message =
moving its `BufSlot` (an ordinary Rust move; `Send` lets it
cross threads). The ring and the pool do not yet compose:
a `BufSlot` cannot travel through a ring slot, and no API
reconstructs buffer access from a bare index — both arrive
with the descriptor-queue cycle. Ring messages this cycle
are by-value `T`s in slots, as before.

**Trust.** Unchanged from the ring: every word in shared
memory is untrusted input. The pool validates the head and
next-link at every pop and fails toward `Exhausted`; a
hostile peer can degrade service (garbage messages, lost
buffers, spurious exhaustion), never cause UB on this side.
input.new_string:
### Usage model: roles and buffer lifecycle

The user-facing contract — who may do what, per object and
per buffer state — lives in the README:
[Usage model](../README.md#usage-model-roles-and-buffer-lifecycle).
The tests exercise exactly what it permits. Summary: ring =
one producer + one consumer; pool = one allocator + any
freers; a buffer is always in exactly one state
(free / allocated / in-flight [vacant until descriptor
queues] / freed) with one permitted toucher; "send" this
cycle means moving the `BufSlot`.

content.text:
**Drop semantics**: they're *available* — I chose not to use them, and it's worth deciding deliberately since this cycle exists to hone the API. `free` is just a CAS push, perfectly legal inside a `Drop` impl; auto-free-on-drop (RAII, iceoryx2's choice for its samples) is implementable today. I went explicit for two reasons:

1. **Symmetry with the ring guards** — `commit`/`release` are explicit acts and drop means "do nothing" (abandon). A `BufSlot` that frees on drop would make drop *mean something* in one guard type and nothing in the others.
2. **The future send path** — descriptor-queue send will consume the guard *without* freeing (ownership goes in-flight). With a `Drop` impl, `free(self)` and `send(self)` both need the `ManuallyDrop`/`mem::forget` dance to move fields out past the destructor — done all the time, but machinery.

The honest counterweight: drop-means-leak is the harsher default for users — forget a `free` on an early-return path and the buffer is gone until re-init, silently. RAII would make the leak impossible and demote explicit `free` to an optimization. Reasonable people pick either; my lean is to keep explicit-free while the API is this young (leaks are visible in tests; `ManuallyDrop` machinery is easier to add than remove), and revisit when send lands. But if the leak-on-drop footgun bothers you more than the asymmetry-with-ring argument, now is the cheapest moment to flip it — say the word.

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**feat: message pool allocator**

Todo #1 picked up: decouple "get a message" from "send it"
with a message pool — self-describing attach-validated
region, fixed-size cache-aligned buffers, intrusive LIFO
free-stack (single-popper Treiber, validated pops), one
owning allocator, anyone frees. Strive for simplicity: the
smallest API that lets us hone the shape and gives iiac-perf
a baseline —

- buffer header is the next-link alone for now; provenance
  (pool id) and any length/type tag land with the
  descriptor-queue cycle, via a pool layout_version bump
  (conscious deferral of the design doc's settle-early note).
- API mirrors the ring: `Pool::init` / `unsafe Pool::attach`,
  a single allocator handle, typed zerocopy access to
  buffers.

Ladder:

- 0.6.0-0 chore: open message pool cycle (done)
- 0.6.0-1 feat: message pool layout + init/attach (done)
- 0.6.0-2 feat: message pool alloc/free (done)
- 0.6.0-3 docs: message pool usage model — roles (ring:
  one producer + one consumer; pool: one allocator + any
  freers), buffer lifecycle states (free → allocated →
  in-flight → freed) and who may touch a buffer in each,
  what "send" means before descriptor queues exist (done)
- 0.6.0-4 test: message pool protocol tests
- 0.6.0 feat: message pool allocator (close-out)

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Descriptor queues: carry `(pool id, buffer offset)`
   descriptors over the existing SPSC ring so any message
   travels over any queue; per-process pool registry to
   resolve ids
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).
2. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
3. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

content.text: Now the picture clicks — the Ring today moves `T`s by value through fixed slots; its real payoff arrives when it carries descriptors and the Pool carries the payloads. Let me add the composed sketch, the app-allocator angle, and then answer the global-allocator question (short version: not as-is, but it's a legitimate destination).

input.old_string:
**What "send" means this cycle.** Transferring a message =
moving its `BufSlot` (an ordinary Rust move; `Send` lets it
cross threads). The ring and the pool do not yet compose:
a `BufSlot` cannot travel through a ring slot, and no API
reconstructs buffer access from a bare index — both arrive
with the descriptor-queue cycle. Ring messages this cycle
are by-value `T`s in slots, as before.
input.new_string:
**What "send" means this cycle.** Transferring a message =
moving its `BufSlot` (an ordinary Rust move; `Send` lets it
cross threads). The ring and the pool do not yet compose:
a `BufSlot` cannot travel through a ring slot, and no API
reconstructs buffer access from a bare index — both arrive
with the descriptor-queue cycle. Ring messages this cycle
are by-value `T`s in slots, as before.

**Where this is heading.** The ring alone is a building
block; the payoff is the two composed — the ring carrying
tiny descriptors while payloads stay put in pool buffers.
Illustrative only (this API does not exist yet; names will
change):

```rust,ignore
// Sender: pool allocator + ring producer.
let mut msg = pool.alloc::<Msg>()?; // get a message
msg.val = 42;                       // fill it in place
producer.send(msg)?;                // consumes the BufSlot;
                                    // only its index travels
                                    // through the ring

// Receiver: ring consumer + freer (other thread or process).
let desc = consumer.reserve_slot::<Desc>()?;
let msg = pool.resolve::<Msg>(&desc)?; // zero-copy view
//                  ... read msg ...
msg.free();     // buffer back to its pool
desc.release(); // ring slot free for the next descriptor
```

One allocation's bytes are written once and never copied —
not by send, not by receive, not across the process
boundary.
